name: Irish Wolfhound
subtitle: Gentle giant of the ancient world
fact: bred to hunting wolves and elk
character: Imposing but friendly demeanor. Gentle giants but pups & young adults are boisterous. Prefer cold weather. Sturdy, yet swift.

training.label: Gentle but independent
traits: Willing and gentle but not driven to work for their owner
Basic obedience comes readily -- advanced work requires patience
Motivated by affection and calm praise more than food
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and therapy work where their gentle nature shines
training.watchOut: Too large for unruly habits to go unchecked -- start lead and recall training immediately

health.generalNote: Irish Wolfhounds have one of the shortest lifespans of any breed. Heart disease and bone cancer are the leading causes of death and significantly affect the breed's average age at death.

conditions.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
conditions.description: The most common cause of death in Irish Wolfhounds. The heart muscle weakens and enlarges, leading to heart failure. Annual cardiac screening from two years of age is essential.

conditions.name: Osteosarcoma
conditions.description: Bone cancer is the second most common cause of death in the breed. The limbs are most commonly affected. Early signs include lameness or swelling and require urgent investigation.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: The enormous deep chest of the Irish Wolfhound creates very high GDV risk. Preventive gastropexy is strongly recommended. Feeding smaller meals and restricting exercise around feeding are essential.

conditions.name: Portosystemic shunt (liver shunt)
conditions.description: An abnormal blood vessel bypasses the liver, causing toxin accumulation. Irish Wolfhounds have an elevated breed risk. Signs include poor growth and neurological episodes after eating.

famousDogs.name: Gelert
famousDogs.knownFor: The legend of Gelert and Llywelyn the Great

lineage.name: Irish Wolfhound
lineage.note: A towering ancient hound bred to hunt wolves and guard halls, and one of the tallest dogs in the world.

children.name: Celtic Hound

children.name: Old hunting dogs of the Celts

children.name: Ancient Molossers

name: Bloodhound
subtitle: The nose that never forgets
fact: can smell a scent trail that is up to 300 hours old
character: Known for expressiveness howling & whining. Patient, and mild-mannered. Especially good with children.
coatColour: Black & Tan, Liver & Tan, or Red

training.label: Scent-driven and stubborn
traits: Extremely intelligent but almost entirely motivated by their nose
Basic obedience is achievable but requires patience and consistency
Recall off lead is unreliable without extensive early training
training.goodFor: Tracking and scent work where their extraordinary nose is an asset
training.watchOut: A Bloodhound on a scent is effectively deaf to commands -- management is key

health.generalNote: Bloodhounds are prone to bloat and ear problems due to their conformation. Their loose skin and drooping ears require regular attention to prevent chronic infections.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: Bloodhounds are among the higher-risk breeds for GDV due to their deep chest. Feeding smaller meals and avoiding exercise around feeding times is essential. Gastropexy is worth considering.

conditions.name: Ear infections
conditions.description: The characteristically long heavy ears create a warm moist environment for bacterial and yeast overgrowth. Weekly cleaning and prompt treatment of any infection are essential.

conditions.name: Entropion and ectropion
conditions.description: Loose facial skin causes drooping or inward-rolling eyelids, leading to chronic eye irritation. Surgical correction may be needed in moderate to severe cases.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis. Hip scoring of breeding animals is recommended.

conditions.name: Skin fold infections
conditions.description: Excessive skin folds, particularly around the face and neck, trap moisture and require regular cleaning to prevent painful bacterial infections.

lineage.name: Bloodhound
lineage.note: The supreme tracking hound, descended from the St Hubert Hound bred by monks in the Ardennes.

children.name: St Hubert Hound
children.note: The abbey scent hound it descends from almost unchanged.

children.name: Old scenting hounds
children.note: The heavy continental tracking hounds of the same line.

name: Mastiff
subtitle: The original gentle protector
fact: can outweigh many a full-grown man
character: Massive, powerful and muscular.  Black mask around the eyes and nose Suspicious of strangers and protective of family.

training.label: Calm but wilful
traits: Intelligent but self-willed and not eager to repeat commands
Responds to a calm, confident handler with patience
Early socialisation is as important as obedience training
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and calm settled behaviour in the home
training.watchOut: Their size makes early training non-negotiable -- an untrained adult Mastiff is unmanageable

health.generalNote: Mastiffs are a giant breed with significant joint disease and a predisposition to certain heart conditions. Their enormous size amplifies the impact of any health problem and shortens average lifespan.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Extremely common in giant breeds. The Mastiff's enormous weight worsens the impact of hip dysplasia significantly. Both parents must be hip-scored before breeding.

conditions.name: Elbow dysplasia
conditions.description: Developmental elbow conditions causing lameness are common in giant breeds. Elbow scoring of breeding animals is strongly recommended.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: The enormous barrel chest of the Mastiff makes GDV a significant risk. Preventive gastropexy is strongly recommended and feeding management is essential.

conditions.name: Osteosarcoma
conditions.description: Bone cancer affects giant breeds at above-average rates. The limbs are most commonly affected and the prognosis is generally poor. Early detection improves outcomes.

conditions.name: Cystinuria
conditions.description: An inherited amino acid transport defect leading to cystine stone formation in the kidneys and bladder. DNA testing is available in some lines.

famousDogs.name: Zorba
famousDogs.knownFor: Former record-holder for heaviest and longest dog

lineage.name: English Mastiff
lineage.note: Britain's ancient war and guard dog, a giant Molosser prized here since Roman times.

children.name: Ancient Molossers
children.note: The huge eastern war and guard dogs at the root of every mastiff. Now extinct.

children.name: Old British bandogs
children.note: The heavy chained guard dogs of early Britain. Now extinct.

name: Great Dane
subtitle: The friendly skyscraper
fact: will tower over humans when stood on hind legs
character: Reserved, Confident, Loving. Considered gentle giants. Powerful with a majestic action. Affectionate and good with children.

training.label: Willing and easy to train
traits: Generally eager to please and picks up commands at a reasonable pace
Responds well to calm, consistent reward-based training
Their size makes early basic training absolutely essential
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and socialisation are the priority given their size
training.watchOut: Jumping up and pulling on lead must be addressed as a puppy -- impossible to manage as an adult

health.generalNote: Great Danes have the shortest average lifespan of any dog breed. Heart disease and bloat are the primary threats. Buyers should understand the significant health responsibilities before committing to this breed.

conditions.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
conditions.description: The most common cause of death in Great Danes. The heart muscle weakens and the chambers enlarge, causing heart failure. Annual cardiac screening from two years of age is essential.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: Great Danes have one of the highest GDV rates of any breed due to their enormous deep chest. Preventive gastropexy is very strongly recommended. GDV is fatal without immediate surgery.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development is very common in giant breeds. The Great Dane's enormous weight makes the consequences particularly severe. Hip scoring of breeding animals is essential.

conditions.name: Wobblers syndrome (Cervical Spondylomyelopathy)
conditions.description: Spinal cord compression in the neck causes a progressive wobbly gait that can advance to paralysis. Great Danes are particularly affected by this condition.

conditions.name: Osteosarcoma
conditions.description: Bone cancer is above-average in giant breeds and Great Danes are particularly at risk. The limbs are most commonly affected. Prognosis is generally poor.

famousDogs.name: Giant George
famousDogs.knownFor: Former Guinness World Record holder for tallest living dog

lineage.name: Great Dane
lineage.note: Germany's giant boarhound, the Deutsche Dogge, built by crossing heavy war mastiffs with tall, fast coursing hounds.

name: Bull Terrier
subtitle: Mischievous clown with a heart of gold
fact: 20% of all white Bull Terriers are deaf
character: Playful but extremely powerful. Active, intelligent, determined with some aggressive tendencies. Generally low-maintenance.
coatColour: White & Fawn, Red, Black or Brindle

training.label: Stubborn but trainable
traits: Intelligent and can be trained but has a strong self-willed streak
Responds best to firm, patient and consistent positive methods
Short sessions work better than long repetitive ones
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and sports that channel their strength
training.watchOut: Early socialisation with other dogs is critical given their tenacity

health.generalNote: Bull Terriers have specific hereditary kidney and heart conditions that require screening. Compulsive behaviours are also more common in this breed and should be considered by prospective owners.

conditions.name: Hereditary Nephritis
conditions.description: A progressive hereditary kidney disease causing renal failure. Urine protein testing of breeding animals can identify affected dogs before they show clinical signs.

conditions.name: Aortic stenosis
conditions.description: Narrowing of the aortic valve restricting blood flow from the heart. Can cause sudden death in severe cases. Cardiac examination before breeding is essential.

conditions.name: Deafness
conditions.description: Hereditary deafness is associated with the white coat and is more prevalent in white Bull Terriers. BAER testing of all puppies before sale is essential.

conditions.name: Compulsive behaviours
conditions.description: Tail chasing and other repetitive behaviours are unusually common in the breed and can escalate to self-injury. Early training intervention and mental stimulation help manage these tendencies.

conditions.name: Skin allergies
conditions.description: Environmental and food allergies causing skin irritation and recurrent infections are common. Management involves identifying and avoiding triggers.

lineage.name: Bull Terrier

name: Doberman Pinscher
subtitle: Intelligent elegance on four legs
fact: worked in the military, police,  search and rescue
character: Dobermanns are often suspicious of strangers. Loyal & Strong. Sleek, muscular body. Needs mental and physical activity

training.label: Very trainable
traits: Intelligent and quick to learn but needs a confident handler
Thrives with clear structure and consistent rules
Strong work ethic once bonded with their owner
training.goodFor: Protection sport, obedience, tracking
training.watchOut: Will test boundaries with a passive owner -- early firm training is essential

health.generalNote: Dobermans are predisposed to two serious heart and neurological conditions that significantly affect lifespan. Proactive cardiac monitoring and DNA testing are essential for all breeding animals.

conditions.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
conditions.description: The most significant health concern in the breed. The heart muscle weakens and the chambers enlarge, leading to heart failure or sudden death. Annual cardiac screening is essential from three years of age.

conditions.name: Wobbler syndrome (Cervical Spondylomyelopathy)
conditions.description: Compression of the spinal cord in the neck causes a characteristic wobbly gait and can progress to paralysis. Surgical and medical management options exist.

conditions.name: Von Willebrand's Disease (vWD)
conditions.description: A hereditary bleeding disorder where blood does not clot properly. Can cause excessive bleeding after injury or surgery. DNA testing identifies affected dogs and carriers.

conditions.name: Hypothyroidism
conditions.description: Underactive thyroid causing weight gain, lethargy, skin problems and coat changes. Manageable with daily oral thyroid hormone supplementation.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis. Hip scoring of breeding animals is recommended as part of a comprehensive health programme.

lineage.name: Doberman Pinscher
lineage.note: A young breed with a known recipe. Created in Germany around 1890 by Louis Dobermann, who wanted a sleek, fearless dog to guard him on his tax rounds.

children.name: German Pinscher

name: Saint Bernard
subtitle: The mountain's most famous rescuer
fact: can smell  20 feet below the surface of the snow
character: Loving, gentle, tolerant and placid. Known to  be exceptionally understanding and patient.  Credited with saving over 2,000 human lives

training.label: Good natured but slow
traits: Willing and gentle but not a quick learner
Responds to patient, calm and reward-based training
Drool and low energy can make training sessions messy and slow
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and search and rescue in alpine contexts historically
training.watchOut: Their size makes pulling and jumping up dangerous -- lead manners must be the first priority

health.generalNote: Saint Bernards have a short average lifespan for a large breed. Heart disease, joint problems and bloat are primary concerns. The breed's enormous size amplifies the health impact of any condition.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Extremely common in the breed. The Saint Bernard's enormous weight dramatically worsens the impact. Both parents must be hip-scored before any breeding.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: The deep barrel chest of the Saint Bernard creates high GDV risk. Preventive gastropexy is strongly recommended. GDV is fatal without immediate surgical intervention.

conditions.name: Osteosarcoma
conditions.description: Bone cancer affects giant breeds at above-average rates and the Saint Bernard is no exception. Early signs of lameness require urgent investigation.

conditions.name: Entropion and ectropion
conditions.description: Drooping eyelids and inward-rolling eyelids are common due to loose facial skin. Both can cause chronic eye irritation and may require surgical correction.

conditions.name: Dilated Cardiomyopathy (DCM)
conditions.description: Heart muscle disease causing heart failure is seen in the breed. Annual cardiac monitoring from middle age is advisable.

lineage.name: Saint Bernard
lineage.note: The Alpine rescue dog of the Great St Bernard hospice, grown from Roman farm mastiffs and later thickened with Newfoundland blood.

children.name: Alpine mastiff farm dogs
children.note: The heavy Roman descended valley dogs, the Sennenhund stock, kept for farm and guard work.

children.name: Newfoundland landrace dogs
children.note: Crossed in during the 1800s after hard winters thinned the hospice line, adding size and coat. Now extinct.

children.name: Ancient Molossers
children.note: The old war dog root every European mastiff traces back to. Now extinct.

name: Afghan Hound
subtitle: Ancient royalty draped in silk
fact: one of  oldest breeds, predating written  history
character: Long, narrow head and muzzle gives refined appearance, yet despite this exhibits silliness. High amount of grooming to exercise needed.

training.label: Aloof and independent
traits: One of the most independently minded of all breeds
Not motivated by pleasing their owner -- training must be on their terms
Short sessions with high-value rewards yield the best results
training.goodFor: Lure coursing where their natural instincts shine
training.watchOut: Never expect reliable recall off lead -- a sighthound chasing prey will not stop

health.generalNote: Afghan Hounds are generally a healthy breed with few major inherited conditions. Their primary health management considerations are anaesthetic sensitivity and maintaining their spectacular coat.

conditions.name: Anaesthetic sensitivity
conditions.description: As a sighthound with very low body fat, Afghan Hounds metabolise certain anaesthetics differently. Only vets experienced with sighthounds should be used for any sedation or surgical procedure.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis. Less common than in heavier breeds but hip scoring of breeding animals is advisable.

conditions.name: Juvenile cataracts
conditions.description: Hereditary cataracts developing in young dogs can significantly impair vision. Eye testing of breeding animals is recommended.

lineage.name: Afghan Hound
lineage.note: An ancient sighthound from the mountains of Afghanistan, coated long against the cold, one of the oldest coursing lines there is.

children.name: Ancient eastern sighthounds
children.note: The slender desert coursing dogs of the old Silk Road, its deepest root. Now extinct.

children.name: Mountain coursing hounds
children.note: The high altitude hunting dogs that gave it the heavy coat and big feet for rough ground. Now extinct.

name: Weimaraner
subtitle: The silver ghost of the hunting field
fact: eyes change colour from blue to amber, gray as they grow
character: Streamlined, athletic and very energetic. Require in excess; exercise, love & attention. Intensely devoted and inteligent.

training.label: Driven and eager to please
traits: Intelligent, energetic and keen to work with their owner
Needs physical exercise before training or focus is impossible
Responds well to consistent reward-based methods
training.goodFor: Field work, tracking, agility and advanced obedience
training.watchOut: Separation anxiety can undermine training if not addressed early

health.generalNote: Weimaraners are a relatively healthy breed but are susceptible to bloat and some hereditary conditions. Their deep chest makes GDV a particular concern that owners should be aware of.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: Weimaraners are among the breeds most at risk of GDV due to their deep narrow chest. The stomach fills with gas and twists, requiring emergency surgery. Preventive gastropexy is recommended.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis and mobility issues. Hip scoring of breeding animals is recommended.

conditions.name: Hypertrophic Osteodystrophy (HOD)
conditions.description: A painful developmental bone disease affecting rapidly growing puppies, causing fever, lameness and swollen joints. Most cases resolve with management but some are severe.

conditions.name: Immune-mediated diseases
conditions.description: Weimaraners appear to have elevated rates of immune-mediated conditions including immune-mediated thrombocytopenia and polyarthritis compared to other breeds.

name: Dalmatian

character: Sleek, muscular body, highly energetic, playful but sensitive. Good with children, although may be too energetic for very small children

health.generalNote: Dalmatians have a unique urinary system that makes them prone to kidney stones, and hereditary deafness affects a significant proportion of the breed. BAER testing of puppies is essential.

conditions.name: Hereditary deafness

conditions.name: Hyperuricosuria and urinary stones

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis and mobility problems. Hip scoring of breeding animals is recommended.

conditions.name: Iris sphincter dysplasia
conditions.description: An iris abnormality causing light sensitivity and vision problems, associated with the same gene complex responsible for the white coat and blue eyes.

children.note: The slender coursing dogs of Egypt and the Near East. Now extinct.

children.name: Old hunting dogs of the Celts
children.note: The native running dogs of Iron Age Europe. Now extinct.

children.name: Ancient eastern sighthounds
children.note: The slender desert coursing dogs of the old world. Now extinct.

children.name: Bloodhound
children.note: A deeper nose for finding game.

children.name: St Hubert Hound
children.note: The abbey scent hound it descends from almost unchanged.

children.name: Old scenting hounds
children.note: The heavy continental tracking hounds of the same line.

children.name: Setter
children.note: Setting-dog blood for style and steadiness. Now in-decline.

children.name: Carriage guard dogs
children.note: The road dogs kept to run with the horses and mind the coach, the job it was made for. Now extinct.

name: Rottweiler
subtitle: Steady, loyal, and utterly fearless
fact: marched alongside Roman legions
character: Big and muscular. Bred to work and self-assured. World-class guardians. Not good for first-time dog owners.

training.label: Very trainable
traits: Intelligent and willing when handler is calm and consistent
Responds well to reward-based training with clear expectations
Naturally protective instincts need channelling early
training.goodFor: Obedience, protection sport, tracking, therapy work
training.watchOut: Stubbornness emerges if rules are inconsistent -- structure from day one is critical

health.generalNote: Rottweilers have significant cancer rates and a predisposition to joint disease and heart conditions. Their large size amplifies the impact of these conditions. Health screening before breeding is essential.

conditions.name: Osteosarcoma
conditions.description: Bone cancer occurs at above-average rates in Rottweilers, particularly in the limbs. Prognosis is poor and treatment is expensive. Early detection through limping or swelling improves outcomes.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development is very prevalent in the breed, causing significant arthritis and mobility impairment. Both parents must be hip-scored before breeding.

conditions.name: Elbow dysplasia
conditions.description: Developmental elbow conditions causing lameness and pain are common in Rottweilers. Elbow scoring of breeding animals is strongly recommended.

conditions.name: Aortic stenosis
conditions.description: Narrowing of the aortic valve restricting blood flow from the heart. Can cause sudden death in severe cases. Cardiac screening before breeding is advised.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: A life-threatening condition where the stomach twists after filling with gas. Large deep-chested breeds including Rottweilers are at elevated risk. Immediate surgery is required.

lineage.name: Rottweiler
lineage.note: The butcher's dog of Rottweil, descended from the drover mastiffs the Roman legions marched over the Alps.

children.name: Ancient Molossers
children.note: The Roman drover and war mastiffs left in the region, the breed's deepest root. Now extinct.

children.name: Local German cattle dogs
children.note: The herding farm dogs of the Wurttemberg valleys it was crossed with to make a steady drover. Now extinct.

name: Old English Sheepdog
subtitle: The lovable mop that herds hearts
fact: it’s not a sheepdog at all
character: Cheerful extroverts. Bold, faithful & trustworthy. Require regular grooming. Need frequent attention, do not like to be alone.
coatColour: Shade of grey, grizzle with white

training.label: Clever and willing
traits: Intelligent herding breed that responds well to positive methods
Can be boisterous which needs channelling early
Thrives with mental stimulation alongside physical exercise
training.goodFor: Obedience, herding activities and agility
training.watchOut: Their exuberance can make focus difficult -- calm consistent sessions work best

health.generalNote: Old English Sheepdogs have some significant inherited health conditions including a drug sensitivity and hip dysplasia. Their thick coat requires regular grooming to prevent skin problems going undetected.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development is prevalent in the breed. Both parents should be hip-scored. The breed's long coat can mask early signs of lameness so vigilance is required.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary retinal degeneration leading to blindness. DNA testing is available and should be carried out on all breeding animals.

conditions.name: Autoimmune conditions
conditions.description: The breed has elevated rates of immune-mediated diseases including autoimmune haemolytic anaemia. Awareness of early signs including sudden lethargy and pale gums is important.

conditions.name: Cerebellar ataxia
conditions.description: A hereditary neurological condition causing progressive loss of coordination. DNA testing is available and has significantly reduced prevalence in tested breeding lines.

famousDogs.name: Digby
famousDogs.knownFor: Digby, the Biggest Dog in the World

lineage.name: Old English Sheepdog
lineage.note: A big, shaggy, bobtailed drover's dog of the west country, bred to drive sheep and cattle to market.

children.name: Bearded Collie
children.note: The shaggy Scottish herder in its make-up.

children.name: Shaggy lowland herders
children.note: Polish Lowland sheepdogs said to have come ashore in Scotland. Now extinct.

children.name: Old working collies
children.note: The old northern hill-collie landrace of Scotland and the borders, the shared working stock the Rough, Smooth and Border collies all grew from. Now extinct.

children.name: Welsh herding dogs
children.note: The old Welsh herding and droving dogs, a long-legged, loose-eyed landrace and the parallel Welsh branch behind the region's sheep-working breeds. Now extinct.

name: Basset Hound
subtitle: Low to the ground, high on charm
fact: have over 220 million smell receptors
character: Sweet-Tempered but can sometimes be stubborn. Built more for endurance than speed.  Powerful Short legs & massive paws.
coatColour: Black & Brown or Red or White

training.label: Leisurely learner
traits: Smart but famously stubborn and not especially eager to please
Scent drive frequently overrides commands
Responds best to very high-value food rewards and short sessions
training.goodFor: Nose work and scent trails which tap into their natural ability
training.watchOut: Patience is essential -- harsh correction causes them to shut down completely

health.generalNote: Basset Hounds are prone to ear infections, obesity and spinal problems due to their conformation. Their long back and heavy body put significant strain on the spine and joints.

conditions.name: Ear infections
conditions.description: Long heavy ears trap moisture and debris, leading to frequent bacterial and yeast infections. Weekly ear cleaning is essential and early treatment prevents chronic problems.

conditions.name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
conditions.description: The long back and heavy body place enormous strain on spinal discs, which can rupture and press on the spinal cord. Weight management is the most important preventive measure.

conditions.name: Obesity
conditions.description: Basset Hounds are very prone to weight gain which massively worsens their spinal and joint problems. Strict portion control and regular low-impact exercise are essential.

conditions.name: Glaucoma
conditions.description: Increased pressure within the eye causes pain and can lead to blindness. Basset Hounds have a hereditary predisposition. Regular eye examinations and prompt treatment are important.

conditions.name: Thrombopathia
conditions.description: A platelet function disorder causing prolonged bleeding. DNA testing is available and affected dogs must be managed carefully around surgery or injury.

lineage.name: Basset Hound
lineage.note: A low set French scenthound bred short in the leg on purpose, so hunters could keep up with it on foot.

children.name: Bloodhound
children.note: The St Hubert scent trailing line behind its nose, long ears and deep voice.

children.name: St Hubert Hound
children.note: The abbey scent hound it descends from almost unchanged.

children.name: Old scenting hounds
children.note: The heavy continental tracking hounds of the same line.

children.name: Basset Artesien Normand
children.note: The French basset breed behind it, later crossed with Bloodhound to make the heavier English Basset. Now endangered.

name: Cavalier King Charles Spaniel
subtitle: Born for the lap, made for the heart
fact: protected owners from the plague
character: Attention-seekers and extremely affectionate. Unique personalities. Some extremely quiet & lazy, others energetic and mischievous.
coatLength: Long silky coat with a little curl

training.label: Gentle and willing
traits: Eager to please and picks up basic commands readily
Responds beautifully to gentle positive methods
Sensitive and bonds deeply with their trainer
training.goodFor: Obedience, therapy work and trick training
training.watchOut: Their desire to be close can make independent work difficult -- build confidence gradually

health.generalNote: Cavaliers have two breed-defining health conditions -- Mitral Valve Disease and Syringomyelia -- that affect the majority of the breed by middle age. Thorough health screening is non-negotiable.

conditions.name: Mitral Valve Disease (MVD)
conditions.description: A progressive heart condition affecting the mitral valve, eventually leading to heart failure. The majority of Cavaliers develop MVD by the age of ten. Breeding protocol requires cardiac screening of both parents.

conditions.name: Syringomyelia and Chiari-like Malformation (CM/SM)
conditions.description: A painful neurological condition where the skull is too small for the brain, causing fluid-filled cavities in the spinal cord. Symptoms include scratching at the neck and sensitivity to touch. MRI screening is recommended.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis and mobility issues. Less prevalent than in larger breeds but still a concern that warrants screening.

conditions.name: Eye problems
conditions.description: Dry eye and inherited retinal conditions can affect the breed. Eye testing of breeding animals is recommended by the Kennel Club.

conditions.name: Episodic Falling Syndrome (EFS)
conditions.description: A muscle hypertonicity disorder causing episodes of rigidity and falling during exercise or excitement. DNA testing identifies affected and carrier dogs.

lineage.name: Cavalier King Charles Spaniel
lineage.note: A 1920s revival of the older, longer-nosed toy spaniel of the Stuart court, bred back from the flat-faced King Charles Spaniel.

children.name: King Charles Spaniel
children.note: The modern flat-faced toy spaniel it was bred back from.

children.name: Old toy spaniels
children.note: The small sporting and lap spaniels of Tudor and Stuart England. Now extinct.

children.name: Asian flat-faced toys
children.note: Pug and oriental toy blood that shortened the muzzle. Now extinct.

children.name: Old sporting toy spaniels
children.note: The longer-muzzled toy spaniels of older paintings. Now extinct.

name: Bulldog
subtitle: Britain's most beloved couch philosopher
fact: Churchill preffered poodles to Bulldogs
character: People-oriented, actively seek human attention. Thick-set but Docile and Friendly, heavy wrinkles and thick folds. Walks with a waddle.

training.label: Determined and slow-paced
traits: Intelligent but stubborn and not particularly eager to please
Tires quickly due to their build -- sessions must be very short
Responds best to very high-value food rewards
training.goodFor: Basic obedience at a relaxed pace
training.watchOut: Overheating is a major risk -- never train in warm conditions or for more than 5-10 minutes

health.generalNote: English Bulldogs have among the highest rates of health problems of any breed due to extreme conformation. Buyers should seek health-tested breeding stock and be prepared for significant veterinary costs.

conditions.name: Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
conditions.description: Severely compromised airways due to the flat face cause chronic breathing difficulty. Many Bulldogs require corrective surgery and remain exercise-intolerant throughout their lives.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Extremely common in the breed. Combined with obesity and breathing restrictions, hip dysplasia can severely limit a Bulldog's quality of life.

conditions.name: Cherry eye
conditions.description: Prolapse of the third eyelid gland requiring surgical correction. Very common in young Bulldogs and may affect both eyes.

conditions.name: Skin fold infections
conditions.description: Deep facial wrinkles trap moisture and debris, leading to chronic bacterial and yeast infections. Daily cleaning of all folds is essential.

conditions.name: Heat intolerance
conditions.description: Bulldogs cannot pant effectively and are at serious risk of heat stroke in even mild warmth. Exercise must be strictly limited in anything above cool temperatures.

conditions.name: Birthing difficulties
conditions.description: The large head relative to body size means most Bulldogs cannot give birth naturally. Caesarean section is required for the vast majority of litters.

lineage.name: Bulldog
lineage.note: Once a fierce bull-baiting dog, then bred into the gentle, wrinkly companion we know after blood sports were banned in 1835.

children.name: Old English Bulldog
children.note: The athletic bull-baiting dog at the root of it all, leaner and fiercer than today's Bulldog. Now extinct.

children.name: Mastiff and Alaunt war dogs
children.note: The big Molossers and the extinct Alaunt that gave it bulk and a broad jaw.

children.name: Old British bandogs
children.note: The heavy butcher's and baiting dogs of old England. Now extinct.

children.name: Mastiff
children.note: The old Molosser guard-and-war dogs that gave the breed its bulk and broad head.

children.name: Ancient Molossers
children.note: The huge eastern war and guard dogs at the root of every mastiff. Now extinct.

children.name: Old British bandogs
children.note: The heavy chained guard dogs of early Britain. Now extinct.

children.name: Pug
children.note: Crossed in during the 1800s to shorten the face and soften the temperament.

children.name: Ancient Chinese toy dogs
children.note: The old Chinese flat-faced lapdogs kept in the imperial court alongside the Pekingese and lion dogs. Now extinct.

children.name: Eastern lion and lap dogs
children.note: The wider family of small eastern companion dogs it shares its roots with. Now extinct.

name: Italian Greyhound
subtitle: Greyhound soul in miniature form
fact: traced back to the Roman Empire
character: Sweet and gentle. Elegant, graceful & quick moving. Can be needy in desire for affection and may appear aloof if not given it.
coatColour: Black, Grey, Chocolate, Fawn, Tan, sable

training.label: Sensitive and curious
traits: Intelligent and willing but easily distracted and sensitive to cold and discomfort
House training can take longer than average
Short positive sessions work best
training.goodFor: Trick training and indoor agility where focus can be maintained
training.watchOut: Never train with anything but gentle positive methods -- they shut down under pressure

health.generalNote: Italian Greyhounds are generally healthy but extremely fragile physically. Bone fractures and dental disease are their most common health challenges. They are also sensitive to anaesthesia.

conditions.name: Bone fractures
conditions.description: The fine bones of the Italian Greyhound are prone to fracture from minor falls or rough play. Particular care is needed around stairs, jumping from heights and interactions with larger dogs.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Severely overcrowded teeth in a narrow jaw make dental disease almost universal in the breed. Daily brushing and regular professional cleaning are non-negotiable.

conditions.name: Anaesthetic sensitivity
conditions.description: As a sighthound with minimal body fat, Italian Greyhounds metabolise certain anaesthetics differently. Only vets experienced with sighthounds should administer any sedation.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness. In a breed with fine delicate legs, patella luxation can cause significant mobility problems.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary retinal degeneration leading to blindness. DNA testing and eye testing of breeding animals are recommended.

lineage.name: Italian Greyhound
lineage.note: A sighthound shrunk to a lapdog in ancient Italy, all the speed in miniature, kept for warmth and company.

children.name: Ancient eastern sighthounds
children.note: The desert coursing hounds of Egypt and the Near East, the Saluki-like deep root found mummified in the pharaohs' tombs. Now extinct.

children.name: Mediterranean miniature sighthounds
children.note: The small coursing dogs bred down as companions across Greece, Rome and Renaissance Italy. Now extinct.

name: Papillon
subtitle: Butterfly ears, boundless spirit
fact: Papillon means butterfly in french
character: Dainty and elegant, with a plumed tail. Outgoing and happy, enjoy sitting in laps most. Thrive on mental stimulation, Natural retrievers.
coatColour: White with Black, Brown, Tan, or Sable

training.label: Exceptionally trainable
traits: One of the most trainable toy breeds -- quick to learn and retains commands well
Thrives on mental stimulation and enjoys complex tasks
Excels in obedience competitions despite their small size
training.goodFor: Competitive obedience, agility and trick training
training.watchOut: Needs sufficient mental exercise or will find its own entertainment

health.generalNote: Papillons are one of the longest-lived toy breeds and are generally healthy. Their main concerns are dental disease and progressive retinal atrophy. Regular dental care is particularly important.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Overcrowded teeth in a small jaw lead to rapid dental disease. Daily brushing and regular professional cleaning are essential throughout the dog's long life.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary retinal degeneration leading to blindness. DNA testing is available and should be carried out on all breeding animals.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness. Surgical correction is available for more severe cases.

conditions.name: Open fontanelle
conditions.description: Some Papillon puppies have an incompletely closed skull at birth. The soft spot may persist and requires care to avoid head trauma in affected individuals.

lineage.name: Papillon
lineage.note: The butterfly dog, a tiny continental spaniel with winged ears, a fixture of European courts for 700 years.

children.name: Continental toy spaniels
children.note: The dwarf spaniels of France, Spain and Italy painted on noble laps through the Renaissance. Now extinct.

children.name: Old European lapdogs
children.note: The wider family of small companion dogs that fed into Europe's toy breeds. Now extinct.

name: Corgi
subtitle: Royal approval, working dog soul
fact: in Welsh Corgi Means “Dwarf Dog”
character: Short, sturdy and low to the ground.  Devoted to their owners, disinterested in strangers. Attractive foxy face.
coatColour: Red, Sable, Fawn, Black and Tan

training.label: Clever and energetic
traits: Intelligent herding breed that is generally willing to work
Responds well to consistent positive training with clear rules
Can be vocal and opinionated -- needs confident handling
training.goodFor: Herding activities, agility and competitive obedience
training.watchOut: Their herding instinct may lead to nipping at heels -- redirect early

health.generalNote: Corgis are prone to spinal disease due to their long back and short legs. Degenerative myelopathy is a significant concern in the breed and DNA testing is strongly recommended.

conditions.name: Degenerative Myelopathy
conditions.description: A progressive fatal spinal cord disease causing gradual hindlimb paralysis. Very prevalent in Pembroke Welsh Corgis. DNA testing identifies at-risk dogs and affected animals should not be bred.

conditions.name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
conditions.description: The long back creates significant spinal disc stress, predisposing Corgis to disc rupture and spinal cord compression. Weight management reduces risk. Surgery can restore function if performed promptly.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis. Hip scoring of breeding animals is recommended.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary retinal degeneration leading to blindness. DNA testing is available and should be used in all breeding animals.

famousDogs.name: Susan
famousDogs.knownFor: Foundation dog of Queen Elizabeth II's Corgi line

famousDogs.name: Muick
famousDogs.knownFor: One of Queen Elizabeth II's final Corgis

lineage.name: Corgi
lineage.note: A big dog in a small body. The Pembroke is a true cattle herder, nipping at heels and ducking the kicks.

children.name: Spitz-type dogs
children.note: Foxy-faced Nordic dogs like the Pomeranian and Swedish Vallhund, brought in by Flemish weavers and Viking settlers.

children.name: Cardigan Welsh Corgi
children.note: Its close cousin and the older of the two Welsh corgis. The pair share deep Welsh roots and were briefly crossed in the 1930s.

children.name: Early badger hunting dogs
children.note: The long, low hunting dogs the Celts are said to have brought to Cardiganshire -- short-legged earth dogs bred to pursue badger and fox. Now extinct.

children.name: Welsh herding dogs
children.note: The old Welsh herding and droving dogs, a long-legged, loose-eyed landrace and the parallel Welsh branch behind the region's sheep-working breeds. Now extinct.

children.name: Welsh herding dogs
children.note: The old Welsh herding and droving dogs, a long-legged, loose-eyed landrace and the parallel Welsh branch behind the region's sheep-working breeds. Now extinct.

name: Bichon Frise
subtitle: The little white cloud of happiness
fact: has European royality connections
character: Cheerful & intelligent, thrive in busy households. Relish being centre of attention. Don’t like being alone for long periods.

training.label: Willing and cheerful
traits: Responds well to positive reward-based methods and loves to perform
Picks up tricks and commands at a good pace
House training can take a little longer than other breeds
training.goodFor: Trick training and obedience -- they love an audience
training.watchOut: House training requires strict routine and crate training from the start

health.generalNote: Bichon Frises are a generally healthy breed but prone to bladder stones, allergies and dental problems. Their white coat can conceal skin conditions that require monitoring.

conditions.name: Bladder stones
conditions.description: Bichons have an elevated tendency to form calcium oxalate bladder stones, causing painful urination and potentially blocking the urethra. Dietary management and regular monitoring are important.

conditions.name: Allergies
conditions.description: Environmental and food allergies causing skin irritation, itching and recurrent ear infections are common in the breed. Management involves identifying and avoiding triggers.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Small jaw and crowded teeth accelerate dental disease. Daily brushing and professional cleaning are essential from an early age.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of place causing intermittent lameness. Surgical correction is available for more severe cases.

lineage.name: Bichon Frise
lineage.note: A fluffy white charmer of the Mediterranean bichon family, carried between ports by sailors and later a favourite in the French court.

children.name: Barbet water dogs
children.note: The curly Barbet, the water dog the little 'barbichon' dogs were bred down from. Now extinct.

children.name: Mediterranean bichon lapdogs
children.note: The old white lapdogs of Malta, Bologna and Tenerife that make up the bichon family. Now extinct.

name: Maltese
subtitle: Ancient companion, timeless elegance
fact: has European royality connections
character: A tufted tail. Gentle, affectionate, intelligent, responsive and trusting. lively, playful, vigorous, and they generally enjoys learning tricks

training.label: Willing and quick
traits: Eager to please and picks up commands readily
Responds well to gentle positive reinforcement
House training takes patience but basic obedience comes easily
training.goodFor: Trick training and obedience at a calm measured pace
training.watchOut: Sensitive to any harshness -- only ever use positive, reward-based methods

health.generalNote: Maltese are a delicate toy breed with several common conditions including dental disease and luxating patella. Their white coat can mask skin problems that require regular monitoring.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Severe dental overcrowding in a tiny jaw leads to rapid tartar accumulation and gum disease. Daily brushing and professional cleaning are essential to prevent pain and tooth loss.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness. Very common in toy breeds. Surgical correction is available for more severe grades.

conditions.name: Portosystemic shunt (liver shunt)
conditions.description: An abnormal blood vessel bypasses the liver, causing toxin accumulation. Causes neurological signs, stunted growth and episodic weakness. Surgical correction can be curative.

conditions.name: White Shaker Syndrome
conditions.description: An immune-mediated condition causing full-body tremors, most commonly in small white dogs. Usually responds well to immunosuppressive treatment with a good prognosis.

lineage.name: Maltese
lineage.note: An ancient white lapdog of the Mediterranean, a favourite of Roman ladies and prized for over two thousand years.

children.name: Ancient Spitz-type dogs
children.note: The small spitz-type dogs many historians see as its oldest ancestors, bred down in size over centuries. Now extinct.

children.name: Mediterranean bichon lapdogs
children.note: The old white bichon-family lapdogs spread around the Mediterranean by ancient traders. Now extinct.

name: Boston Terrier
subtitle: The American gentleman in a tuxedo
fact: Previously pit-fighting and rat hunter
character: Affectionate, Good-natured, playful.  Enjoy people & get along well with children.  Small and erect “bat” like ears.

training.label: Eager and quick
traits: Intelligent and generally keen to please their owner
Responds well to positive reward-based training
Can be stubborn at times but responds to consistency
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and trick training -- they enjoy showing off
training.watchOut: Sensitive to tone -- always keep sessions positive and upbeat

health.generalNote: Boston Terriers are a brachycephalic breed with several health challenges. Their protruding eyes and flat face create specific vulnerabilities that owners need to manage proactively.

conditions.name: Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
conditions.description: Restricted airways due to the flat face cause breathing difficulty and exercise intolerance. Many Boston Terriers benefit from surgical correction of narrowed nostrils and elongated soft palate.

conditions.name: Corneal ulcers
conditions.description: Prominent eyes are prone to corneal injury and ulceration. Even minor scratches require prompt veterinary attention to prevent serious eye damage.

conditions.name: Hemi-vertebrae
conditions.description: Malformed vertebrae, particularly in the tail region, can cause spinal cord compression leading to weakness and incontinence. Severity varies considerably between individuals.

conditions.name: Deafness
conditions.description: Hereditary deafness is associated with white markings and blue eyes in the breed. BAER testing of all puppies before sale is recommended.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness. Surgical correction is available for more severe grades.

famousDogs.name: Sergeant Stubby
famousDogs.knownFor: First World War decorated war dog

lineage.name: Boston Terrier
lineage.note: An American original from 1870s Boston, traced back to a single dog, Hooper's Judge, a bulldog and terrier cross bred smaller and smarter.

children.name: Bulldog
children.note: Brought the stocky frame and the easy-going, affectionate streak.

children.name: Eastern lion and lap dogs

children.name: White English Terrier
children.note: The now-extinct terrier behind the neat build and lively mind.

name: Beagle
subtitle: Nose first, heart second, ears last
fact: bred to catch rabbits & hares
character: Can be very noisy. Even-tempered, curious. Keen to be involved, can get carried away with playfulness. Can jump walls & dig under fences.

training.label: Nose-led and stubborn
traits: Highly intelligent but motivated by scent above all else
Once on a scent trail, recall becomes very difficult
Responds to training best when food rewards are high value
training.goodFor: Scent work and nose games where their instinct works for you
training.watchOut: Keep on lead in open spaces until recall is rock solid -- which may take months

health.generalNote: Beagles are generally a healthy and robust breed but carry some inherited conditions including epilepsy and certain eye diseases. Their deep love of food makes obesity a persistent management challenge.

conditions.name: Epilepsy
conditions.description: Idiopathic epilepsy has a hereditary component in Beagles, with seizures typically beginning in young adulthood. Usually manageable with anti-epileptic medication.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis. Less prevalent than in larger breeds but still worth screening in breeding animals.

conditions.name: Musladin-Lueke Syndrome (MLS)
conditions.description: A hereditary connective tissue disease unique to Beagles causing tight skin, abnormal gait and shortened outer toes. DNA testing identifies carriers.

conditions.name: Obesity
conditions.description: Beagles are highly food-motivated and among the breeds most prone to obesity. Strict portion control and regular exercise are essential to prevent weight-related health problems.

conditions.name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
conditions.description: Degenerated discs pressing on the spinal cord cause pain and can lead to paralysis. Beagles are at moderate risk and weight management helps reduce the likelihood.

lineage.name: Beagle
lineage.note: A small, merry pack hound bred to hunt hare on foot, with a big voice and a busy nose.

children.name: Talbot hound
children.note: The white Norman hound at the root of the running packs.

children.name: Southern Hound
children.note: Deep-nosed scent-hound blood.

children.name: Talbot hounds
children.note: The white Norman hound at its root.

children.name: St Hubert Hound
children.note: The abbey scent hounds brought across the Channel.

children.name: Greyhound
children.note: A little sighthound for speed and a tidy build.

children.name: Celtic Hound

children.name: Old hunting dogs of the Celts
children.note: The native running dogs of Iron Age Europe. Now extinct.

children.name: Ancient eastern sighthounds
children.note: The slender desert coursing dogs of the old world. Now extinct.

name: Siberian Husky
subtitle: Born to run, impossible to contain
fact: Bread by a tribe in northeast Asia over 3,000 years ago
character: Larger than life mischievous characters.  Best known for occupation as a sled dog. Tend not to bark but do howl.
coatColour: All colours from black to pure white

training.label: Independent minded
traits: Intelligent but bred to make their own decisions on the trail
Recall is notoriously unreliable without very early training
Gets bored quickly with repetitive obedience drills
training.goodFor: Canicross, skijoring, and activities that use their natural drive
training.watchOut: Never rely on off-lead recall in an unsecured area -- strong prey drive

health.generalNote: Siberian Huskies are one of the healthier breeds overall but do have inherited eye conditions and hip dysplasia in their lineage. Health testing of breeding stock is recommended.

conditions.name: Hereditary Cataracts
conditions.description: A hereditary form of cataracts that can affect both eyes and lead to significantly impaired vision. Eye testing of breeding animals is mandatory under the Kennel Club health scheme.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary retinal degeneration leading to night blindness and eventual loss of sight. DNA testing is available to identify carriers before breeding.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip joint development causing arthritis. Less prevalent than in many working breeds but worth screening in breeding animals.

conditions.name: Zinc-responsive dermatosis
conditions.description: A skin condition where the dog cannot absorb sufficient zinc, causing crusty lesions around the muzzle and eyes. Responds well to zinc supplementation.

lineage.name: Siberian Husky
lineage.note: The sled dog of the Chukchi people of north east Siberia, bred to pull light loads vast distances in brutal cold.

children.name: Chukchi sled dogs
children.note: The endurance team dogs of the Siberian Arctic, the breed almost unchanged from them. Now extinct.

children.name: Ancient Arctic spitz
children.note: The wider northern spitz family behind the pricked ears, curled tail and dense double coat. Now extinct.

name: Shih Tzu
subtitle: Little lion, maximum affection
fact: name translates as ‘little lion’
character: Outgoing, happy, playful & trusting.  Loves being around people. Suited for owners who can be home a lot. Serious grooming time.

training.label: Charming but stubborn
traits: Intelligent but bred as a companion rather than a working dog
Can be wilful and responds better to short positive sessions
House training can take longer than average
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and trick training at a relaxed pace
training.watchOut: Patience is essential -- they will simply walk away from training they find boring

health.generalNote: Shih Tzus are a brachycephalic breed with several health challenges related to their flat face and compact body. Eye problems and breathing difficulties are particularly common.

conditions.name: Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
conditions.description: Restricted airways cause breathing difficulty, snoring and exercise intolerance. Many Shih Tzus benefit from corrective surgery to improve airflow and quality of life.

conditions.name: Eye problems
conditions.description: Prominent eyes are vulnerable to corneal ulcers, dry eye and injury. Daily eye cleaning is essential and any redness or discharge requires prompt veterinary attention.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Overcrowded teeth in a small flat jaw accelerate dental disease. Daily brushing and professional cleaning are important from an early age.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis and mobility issues. Less common than in larger breeds but worth screening in breeding animals.

conditions.name: Renal dysplasia
conditions.description: A hereditary kidney abnormality causing progressive renal failure, often in young dogs. No cure exists. DNA-testing awareness within breeding programmes is growing.

lineage.name: Shih Tzu
lineage.note: The lion dog of the Chinese imperial court, made by crossing Tibetan holy dogs with the palace's own toys.

children.name: Tibetan temple dogs

children.name: Ancient Chinese toy dogs
children.note: The flat faced palace lapdogs, the Pekingese side, it was bred with behind the walls. Now extinct.

name: Jackapoo
subtitle: Jack Russell fire meets Poodle brains
fact: cross Bred from Jack Russell Terrier and Poodle
character: Sizes can vary. Happy, even-tempered with cheeky personalities. Great with families.  Keen to chase and investigate.

training.label: Quick but wilful

Benefits from Poodle focus but retains Jack Russell independence
Responds well to high-value rewards and varied short sessions
training.goodFor: Agility, trick training and obedience
training.watchOut: Prey drive from the terrier side needs careful management off lead

health.generalNote: Jackapoos can inherit conditions from Jack Russell Terriers and Poodles. Lens luxation from the Jack Russell side is a particularly important concern that should be DNA tested in parent animals.

conditions.name: Primary Lens Luxation (PLL)
conditions.description: Inherited from the Jack Russell Terrier parent. The lens dislocates causing acute pain and glaucoma requiring emergency treatment. DNA testing of the Jack Russell parent is essential.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Can be inherited from either parent breed. DNA testing of both parents is strongly recommended before breeding.

conditions.name: Legg-Calve-Perthes Disease
conditions.description: Can be inherited from the Jack Russell parent. Degeneration of the femoral head causes hip pain in young dogs. Surgical correction provides excellent long-term results.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: Can be inherited from either parent. The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness.

conditions.name: Mitral Valve Disease (MVD)
conditions.description: Inherited from the Cavalier parent, MVD causes progressive heart failure affecting the majority of Cavaliers by middle age. Cardiac screening of the Cavalier parent is essential.

conditions.name: Syringomyelia (CM/SM)
conditions.description: A painful neurological condition that can be inherited from the Cavalier parent. Responsible breeders MRI screen their Cavalier breeding animals.

conditions.name: Allergies
conditions.description: Inherited from the Bichon side, environmental and food allergies causing skin irritation and ear problems are relatively common.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness. Inherited from either parent. Surgical correction is available for moderate to severe cases.

lineage.name: Cavachon

name: Miniature Schnauzer
subtitle: Big personality in a compact package
fact: bred to catch rats in germany. Schnauzer means beard
character: Bushy eyebrows, a walrus-like moustache. Sturdy, alert, courageous dogs. Enjoy vigorous play. Happy in a city apartment or the country.

training.label: Smart and willing
traits: Highly intelligent and picks up commands quickly
Can have a stubborn streak but responds well to clear consistent rules
Food motivated which makes reward-based training effective
training.goodFor: Obedience, agility and earthdog activities that use their terrier instincts
training.watchOut: Their vocal nature needs addressing early -- barking can become a default behaviour

health.generalNote: Miniature Schnauzers are prone to specific metabolic conditions including pancreatitis and hyperlipidaemia, making diet management particularly important for this breed.

conditions.name: Hyperlipidaemia
conditions.description: Elevated blood fat levels predispose Miniature Schnauzers to pancreatitis and other complications. A low-fat diet and regular blood monitoring are important management tools.

conditions.name: Pancreatitis
conditions.description: Inflammation of the pancreas causing severe vomiting, pain and potentially fatal complications. Linked to the breed's tendency toward high blood fat levels. Strict low-fat diet is essential.

conditions.name: Bladder stones
conditions.description: Both calcium oxalate and struvite stones are common in the breed. Dietary management and regular monitoring can help prevent recurrence after treatment.

conditions.name: Myotonia congenita
conditions.description: A hereditary muscle condition causing muscle stiffness and difficulty rising. DNA testing identifies carriers and affected dogs.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary retinal degeneration leading to progressive vision loss. DNA testing is available and should be carried out on breeding animals.

lineage.name: Miniature Schnauzer

name: Greyhound

coatColour: White, Fawn, Gray, Blue, Black, Brindle

Responds well to calm, reward-based training without pressure
Ex-racing dogs often need patience to learn basic pet commands

training.watchOut: Off-lead exercise only in fully enclosed spaces -- prey drive is extremely strong

health.generalNote: Greyhounds are a healthy breed but require particular care around anaesthesia and are prone to dental disease. Ex-racing dogs may have specific health histories that require attention.

conditions.name: Anaesthetic sensitivity
conditions.description: Greyhounds metabolise certain anaesthetics very slowly due to their low body fat and unique liver enzymes. Only vets experienced with sighthounds should administer anaesthesia to this breed.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Greyhounds have unusually high rates of dental disease, particularly periodontal disease. Daily brushing and regular professional cleaning are particularly important for this breed.

conditions.name: Osteosarcoma
conditions.description: Bone cancer occurs at higher rates in large breeds including Greyhounds. Early signs include persistent lameness or swelling. Prognosis is generally poor but early detection helps.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: The deep narrow chest of the Greyhound creates elevated GDV risk. Feeding smaller meals and avoiding exercise around feeding times significantly reduces the likelihood.

lineage.name: Greyhound
lineage.note: The fastest of all dogs, a sighthound that has hunted in Britain since antiquity.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: Deep-chested Lurchers are at elevated risk of GDV. Feeding smaller meals and avoiding exercise immediately after feeding significantly reduces the risk.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Lurchers with Greyhound or Whippet ancestry inherit a predisposition to dental disease. Regular brushing and professional cleaning are important from an early age.

lineage.name: Lurcher
lineage.note: Not a breed but a type: a sighthound crossed with a working dog, long the poacher's and traveller's companion, fast and quiet.

conditions.name: Anaesthetic sensitivity

name: Cockapoo
subtitle: Britain's favourite hybrid with good reason
fact: cross Bred from Cocker Spaniel & Poodle
character: 3 Size Varities; Toy, Mini & Standard. Appearance can vary. Extremely friendly, easily trained. Fun-loving with plenty of energy.

training.label: Eager and quick
traits: Inherits willingness from both parent breeds
Picks up commands readily with positive reward-based methods
Thrives on mental stimulation alongside physical exercise
training.goodFor: Obedience, agility and therapy work
training.watchOut: Separation anxiety is common -- independence training should start from day one

health.generalNote: Cockapoos can inherit health conditions from both parent breeds -- Cocker Spaniels and Poodles. Health testing of both parents before breeding significantly reduces inherited risks.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Inherited from both parent breeds, PRA causes gradual retinal degeneration and eventual blindness. DNA testing of both parents is strongly recommended before breeding any Cockapoo.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Can be inherited from the Cocker Spaniel parent. Hip scoring of both parents is advisable for responsible breeding.

conditions.name: Ear infections
conditions.description: Inherited from the Cocker Spaniel side, floppy ears and a dense coat create conditions for recurring ear infections. Regular ear cleaning is essential.

conditions.name: Addison's disease
conditions.description: Can be inherited from the Poodle parent. The adrenal glands fail causing episodes of collapse. Manageable with lifelong hormone supplementation.

lineage.name: Cockapoo
lineage.note: One of the original designer crosses, around since the 1960s. Bred to pair the Cocker's friendly nature with the Poodle's low-shedding coat.

children.name: Cocker Spaniel
children.note: Brings the merry, people-loving temperament and those soft spaniel looks.

children.name: Land spaniels

children.name: Field Spaniel

children.name: Land spaniels

children.name: Clumber Spaniel

children.name: Basset and heavy hounds

name: West Highland Terrier
subtitle: Small, white, and absolutely certain
fact: Easily gets burned on sunny days
character: Inquisitive, cheerful playmate. Distinctive white coat. Diminutive but sturdy with a compact body. A carrot-shaped tail wagging.

training.label: Independent terrier
traits: Smart but self-willed with a classic terrier attitude
Responds to training on their own terms -- must be kept interesting
Short sessions with high-value rewards work best
training.goodFor: Earthdog activities, agility and basic obedience
training.watchOut: Prey drive and stubbornness means recall must be trained obsessively from puppyhood

health.generalNote: West Highland White Terriers are strongly associated with skin allergies and inflammatory bowel disease. Their distinctive white coat requires regular attention and can mask underlying skin conditions.

conditions.name: Atopic dermatitis
conditions.description: Chronic skin allergy causing intense itching, recurrent skin infections and ear problems. The breed is among those most affected. Long-term management with medication and allergen avoidance is typically required.

conditions.name: Westie Lung Disease (Pulmonary Fibrosis)
conditions.description: A progressive and ultimately fatal lung condition causing scarring of the lung tissue. Exercise intolerance and breathlessness worsen over time. There is no cure.

conditions.name: Craniomandibular Osteopathy (CMO)
conditions.description: Excessive bone growth of the lower jaw in growing puppies causes extreme pain when eating. Most cases resolve after skeletal maturity but feeding is severely impaired during development.

conditions.name: Copper toxicosis
conditions.description: Abnormal copper accumulation in the liver causes progressive liver damage. Dietary management and medication can slow progression. DNA testing awareness is growing.

lineage.name: West Highland Terrier
lineage.note: Bred from the white pups of the Highland working terriers, kept apart on purpose so they would show up against the heather and rock.

children.name: Cairn Terrier
children.note: The closest cousin, and the main source of the Westie's plucky working type.

children.name: Skye terrier stock
children.note: The island working terriers it was once grouped with as a 'short-haired Skye'. Now endangered.

children.name: Highland mainland terriers
children.note: The mainland Highland branch of the same Skye earth-dog stock, shared with the Scottie and Westie. Now extinct.

children.name: Scottish Terrier
children.note: Another of the old Highland terriers sharing the same rugged roots.

children.name: Old Highland terriers
children.note: The shared Highland working-terrier stock, once all just called Scottish terriers. Now extinct.

children.name: Skye terrier stock
children.note: The long-coated island terriers from the same rootstock. Now endangered.

children.name: Skye Terrier
children.note: Part of the same west-coast terrier family that shaped the breed.

children.name: Old Highland terriers
children.note: The old working-terrier stock of the Highlands that every Scottish terrier springs from. Now extinct.

children.name: Skye terrier stock
children.note: The long-coated Isle of Skye earth dogs at the root of the whole Highland terrier family. Now endangered.

name: Pomeranian
subtitle: Tiny dog, enormous opinion
fact: survived the sinking of the Titanic
character: Extroverted & Lively. Personality of much bigger dog. Coat extending over chest & shoulders. Mini relation of powerful sled dogs.

training.label: Bright and willing
traits: Intelligent and keen to please -- learns commands quickly
Thrives with mental stimulation and enjoys learning tricks
Responds well to gentle positive methods
training.goodFor: Trick training and obedience -- their sharp minds love a challenge
training.watchOut: Can be vocal and difficult to settle -- impulse control training should start early

health.generalNote: Pomeranians are generally a healthy breed but prone to alopecia X, luxating patella and tracheal collapse. Their dense coat requires careful monitoring for hidden skin conditions.

conditions.name: Alopecia X (Black Skin Disease)
conditions.description: A cosmetic condition causing bilateral hair loss and hyperpigmentation of the skin. The cause is poorly understood but it does not cause the dog physical discomfort. Affected dogs should not be bred.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness. Very common in toy breeds and Pomeranians in particular. Surgical correction is available for moderate to severe cases.

conditions.name: Tracheal collapse
conditions.description: Weakening of the tracheal rings causes airway collapse and a characteristic honking cough. Use of a harness rather than collar reduces neck pressure and helps manage symptoms.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Overcrowded teeth in a small jaw lead to rapid dental disease. Daily brushing and regular professional cleaning are essential to prevent painful gum disease and tooth loss.

lineage.name: Pomeranian
lineage.note: A spitz shrunk to a toy in the Pomerania region, descended from big Arctic sled and herding dogs.

children.name: Arctic sled spitz
children.note: The large Nordic sled, hunting and herding spitz of Iceland and Lapland, the breed's deep northern root.

children.name: German farm spitz
children.note: The Wolfspitz and mid-size German Spitz guard strains of the Pomerania region it was bred straight down from. Now extinct.

name: French Bulldog
subtitle: Urban legend, actual best friend
fact: Frenchies Are Prone to Obesity
character: Thrives on attention, deeply affectionate.  Full of courage, with clown-like qualities. Known to snore. Naturally bat eared.

training.label: Willing and playful
traits: Generally eager to please and responds well to reward-based training
Learns commands at a reasonable pace with short positive sessions
Sensitive to temperature -- sessions must be kept brief
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and trick training in short fun sessions
training.watchOut: Overheating risk means training must always be indoors or in cool conditions

health.generalNote: French Bulldogs are a brachycephalic breed with significant health challenges arising from their flat face and compact body. Prospective owners should research these carefully before purchasing.

conditions.name: Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
conditions.description: A cluster of airway abnormalities including narrowed nostrils, elongated soft palate and narrow trachea that restrict breathing. Many French Bulldogs require corrective surgery to breathe comfortably.

conditions.name: Spinal problems (hemivertebrae)
conditions.description: Malformed vertebrae can cause spinal cord compression leading to pain, weakness and incontinence. Severity varies from mild to life-altering.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip joint development causing pain and mobility issues, compounded by the breed's already restricted exercise capacity.

conditions.name: Cherry eye
conditions.description: Prolapse of the gland of the third eyelid, appearing as a red mass in the corner of the eye. Requires surgical correction and can recur.

conditions.name: Skin fold dermatitis
conditions.description: Bacterial or yeast infections in the skin folds around the face and tail. Requires regular cleaning and can be chronic.

conditions.name: Heat stroke
conditions.description: Due to their compromised airways, French Bulldogs cannot regulate body temperature effectively and are extremely vulnerable to heat stroke. Exercise in warm weather can be fatal.

lineage.name: French Bulldog
lineage.note: Born when English lacemakers took their little toy bulldogs to France, where Paris fell for the bat eared result.

children.name: Bulldog
children.note: The English toy bulldogs the Nottingham lace workers carried across to Normandy.

children.name: Old English Bulldog
children.note: The athletic bull-baiting dog at the root of it all, leaner and fiercer than today's Bulldog. Now extinct.

children.name: Mastiff and Alaunt war dogs
children.note: The big Molossers and the extinct Alaunt that gave it bulk and a broad jaw.

children.name: Old British bandogs
children.note: The heavy butcher's and baiting dogs of old England. Now extinct.

children.name: Mastiff
children.note: The old Molosser guard-and-war dogs that gave the breed its bulk and broad head.

children.name: Ancient Molossers
children.note: The huge eastern war and guard dogs at the root of every mastiff. Now extinct.

children.name: Old British bandogs
children.note: The heavy chained guard dogs of early Britain. Now extinct.

children.name: Pug
children.note: Crossed in during the 1800s to shorten the face and soften the temperament.

children.name: Ancient Chinese toy dogs
children.note: The old Chinese flat-faced lapdogs kept in the imperial court alongside the Pekingese and lion dogs. Now extinct.

children.name: Eastern lion and lap dogs
children.note: The wider family of small eastern companion dogs it shares its roots with. Now extinct.

children.name: Parisian ratters and terriers
children.note: The city ratting dogs of Paris crossed in, thought to fix the upright bat ears. Now extinct.

name: Staffordshire Bull Terrier
subtitle: The nanny dog that loves unconditionally
fact: bred for combat against much big animals
character: Reputation of a child’s playmate and guardian. Powerfully agile with Huge muscles.  A lover, not a fighter. Gentle and docile.
coatColour: Black, White, Brindle, Fawn, Red, Blue

training.label: People-pleasing and trainable
traits: Eager to please their owner and responds well to reward-based training
Highly food and toy motivated making sessions productive
Learns quickly when training is kept fun and upbeat
training.goodFor: Obedience, agility and therapy work where their affectionate nature shines
training.watchOut: Dog-to-dog manners need careful early work given their terrier heritage

health.generalNote: Staffies are generally a robust breed but carry L-2-Hydroxyglutaric Aciduria and hereditary cataracts. DNA testing has made it possible to eliminate these conditions from breeding lines.

conditions.name: L-2-Hydroxyglutaric Aciduria (L2HGA)
conditions.description: A hereditary metabolic disorder causing progressive neurological deterioration including seizures, incoordination and behavioural changes. DNA testing is available and eliminates the condition from tested breeding lines.

conditions.name: Hereditary cataracts
conditions.description: Hereditary cataracts cause cloudiness of the lens and can impair vision. Eye testing and DNA testing of breeding animals significantly reduces prevalence.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis and reduced mobility. Hip scoring of breeding animals is recommended.

conditions.name: Skin allergies
conditions.description: Environmental and food allergies are relatively common, causing recurrent skin infections and ear problems. Management involves identifying and avoiding relevant allergens.

famousDogs.name: Sox
famousDogs.knownFor: PDSA Order of Merit recipient, police dog

lineage.name: Staffordshire Bull Terrier
lineage.note: A classic bull-and-terrier, bred in the Black Country by crossing the old bulldog with game local terriers for grip and gameness.

children.name: Bulldog
children.note: The old, athletic bull-baiting type, not the modern show dog, for strength and courage.

children.name: Old English Bulldog
