children.name: Old British bandogs
children.note: The heavy chained guard dogs of early Britain. Now extinct.

children.name: Pug
children.note: Crossed in during the 1800s to shorten the face and soften the temperament.

children.name: Ancient Chinese toy dogs
children.note: The old Chinese flat-faced lapdogs kept in the imperial court alongside the Pekingese and lion dogs. Now extinct.

children.name: Eastern lion and lap dogs
children.note: The wider family of small eastern companion dogs it shares its roots with. Now extinct.

children.name: Old English Black and Tan Terrier
children.note: Added speed, agility and terrier fire to the heavier bulldog base.

name: Chihuahua
subtitle: Ancient soul in the world's smallest body
fact: appear on artifacts of past civilizations
character: Chatty & vocal. Bonds closely with owner/s.  Alert & curious but prone to separation anxiety. Prickly around strangers & can appear nervous.

training.label: Clever but opinionated
traits: Surprisingly intelligent but prone to small-dog syndrome if indulged
Can be difficult to house train -- patience and consistency required
Responds well to training when taken seriously as a proper dog
training.goodFor: Trick training and obedience where their sharp minds engage
training.watchOut: Owners who skip training because they are small create difficult dogs -- treat them like a big dog

health.generalNote: Chihuahuas are generally hardy for their size but are prone to dental problems, luxating patella and heart disease. Their fragile build also makes them vulnerable to trauma.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Severely overcrowded teeth in a tiny jaw cause rapid tartar build-up, gum disease and early tooth loss. Daily brushing and professional dental cleaning are essential from an early age.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips in and out of position causing lameness. Very common in toy breeds. Surgical correction is available for moderate to severe cases.

conditions.name: Mitral Valve Disease (MVD)
conditions.description: Progressive heart disease affecting the mitral valve is very common in older Chihuahuas and a leading cause of death in the breed. Regular cardiac monitoring from middle age is advisable.

conditions.name: Hydrocephalus
conditions.description: Fluid on the brain causes an enlarged domed skull, seizures and neurological signs. Apple-headed Chihuahuas are at particular risk. Severe cases require surgical shunting.

conditions.name: Hypoglycaemia
conditions.description: Tiny toy breed puppies are highly susceptible to dangerously low blood sugar. Frequent small meals and avoidance of stress are essential during the first few months of life.

lineage.name: Chihuahua
lineage.note: The smallest dog of all, traced to the Techichi companion dogs kept by the peoples of ancient Mexico.

children.name: Ancient Techichi dogs
children.note: The small sacred companion dogs of the Toltec and Aztec, the breed's direct root.

children.name: Small imported dogs
children.note: Tiny dogs brought by later traders, thought to add coat and the bold, terrier like spark. Now extinct.

name: German Shepherd
subtitle: Brains, bravery, and boundless loyalty
fact: Have 2 Stars on The ‘Walk of Fame’ in hollywood
character: Protective, affectionate & energetic.  A Desire to work, excellent at search & rescue. Often therapy and service dogs.

training.label: Highly trainable
traits: Focused, driven and quick to learn when properly motivated
Bonds deeply with one handler which aids consistency
Excels at complex multi-step tasks
training.goodFor: Police, military, search and rescue, protection sport
training.watchOut: Needs firm, consistent handling from day one -- inconsistency creates anxiety

health.generalNote: German Shepherds are prone to several significant hereditary conditions, most notably degenerative myelopathy and hip dysplasia. Thorough health screening of breeding stock is essential.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: One of the breeds most affected by hip dysplasia, causing progressive arthritis and mobility impairment. Both parents must be hip-scored. Weight management and physiotherapy help manage the condition.

conditions.name: Degenerative Myelopathy
conditions.description: A progressive spinal cord disease causing gradual paralysis of the hind limbs. There is no cure. DNA testing identifies at-risk dogs and affected breeding animals should not be bred from.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: A life-threatening emergency where the stomach fills with gas and twists. Requires immediate surgical intervention. German Shepherds are among the higher-risk breeds.

conditions.name: Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency (EPI)
conditions.description: The pancreas fails to produce sufficient digestive enzymes, leading to dramatic weight loss despite a ravenous appetite. Manageable with enzyme supplements added to food.

conditions.name: Perianal Fistula
conditions.description: Painful ulcerating tunnels around the anal region that can cause significant distress and difficulty defecating. Treatment involves immunosuppressive medication and sometimes surgery.

lineage.name: German Shepherd
lineage.note: Standardised from Germany's regional sheep herding dogs into one clever, hard working breed in the 1890s.

children.name: Thuringian herding dogs
children.note: The pricked ear, curl tailed herders of central Germany behind the alert look. Now extinct.

children.name: Wurttemberg sheepdogs
children.note: The larger, steadier southern herding dogs that gave size and a calm working head. Now extinct.

children.name: Old German farm guards
children.note: Local farm and guard stock folded in as the breed was fixed to a single type. Now extinct.

name: Pug
subtitle: Maximum personality, minimum snout
fact: a long history of royal positions
character: Distinctive curled tail and flat-faced muzzel. Full of character. Playful, with lots of energy. Highly adaptable & sociable.

training.label: Food-motivated but stubborn
traits: Responds well to food rewards but tires quickly in the heat
Can be selectively deaf when not sufficiently motivated
House training takes patience and strict routine
training.goodFor: Basic obedience and trick training at a slow measured pace
training.watchOut: Overheating is a serious risk -- keep sessions very short especially in warm weather

health.generalNote: Pugs face significant health challenges due to their brachycephalic conformation. The breed has one of the shortest average lifespans among small dogs. Careful consideration is essential before purchase.

conditions.name: Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
conditions.description: Severely restricted airways cause chronic breathing difficulty, snoring and exercise intolerance. Corrective surgery can improve quality of life significantly but does not fully resolve the underlying anatomy.

conditions.name: Pug Dog Encephalitis (PDE)
conditions.description: A fatal breed-specific inflammatory brain disease unique to Pugs causing seizures, circling and death. Genetic testing can identify at-risk dogs. There is no cure.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing pain and arthritis, compounded by the breed's tendency toward obesity which further stresses the joints.

conditions.name: Eye problems
conditions.description: Protruding eyes are vulnerable to ulcers, dry eye and proptosis following minor head trauma. Daily eye checks are recommended.

conditions.name: Skin fold dermatitis
conditions.description: Infections in facial folds require daily cleaning to prevent painful chronic skin disease. The nose fold in particular needs regular attention.

conditions.name: Hemi-vertebrae
conditions.description: Malformed vertebrae in the screw tail and spine can cause spinal cord compression, weakness and incontinence. Severity ranges from mild to requiring surgery.

lineage.name: Pug
lineage.note: A comical, flat-faced toy from ancient China, bred as a companion for emperors before Dutch traders carried it to Europe.

children.name: Ancient Chinese toy dogs
children.note: The old Chinese flat-faced lapdogs kept in the imperial court alongside the Pekingese and lion dogs. Now extinct.

children.name: Eastern lion and lap dogs
children.note: The wider family of small eastern companion dogs it shares its roots with. Now extinct.

name: Labradoodle
subtitle: The original designer dog done right
fact: cross Bred from Labrador &  Poodle
character: 3 Size Varities; Toy, Medium & Standard.  Active and intelligent. Adorable looks and friendly personalities. Great family companions.
coatLength: 3 types of coats: Wool, Fleece & Hair

training.label: Eager and intelligent
traits: Combines Labrador willingness with Poodle intelligence
Learns commands quickly and responds well to varied training
Highly food and toy motivated
training.goodFor: Assistance work, obedience, agility and therapy dogs
training.watchOut: Their energy means exercise before training is essential for a focused session

health.generalNote: Labradoodles can inherit conditions from both Labradors and Poodles. Health testing of both parents significantly reduces inherited risks and should always be requested from responsible breeders.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Can be inherited from the Labrador parent. Hip scoring of both parents before breeding is essential to reduce risk in offspring.

conditions.name: Elbow dysplasia
conditions.description: Can be inherited from the Labrador parent. Elbow scoring of both parents is recommended by responsible breeders.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Can be inherited from either parent breed. DNA testing of both parents for their respective breed-specific PRA variants is strongly recommended.

conditions.name: Addison's disease
conditions.description: Inherited from the Poodle side. The adrenal glands fail to produce sufficient hormones. Manageable with lifelong supplementation.

conditions.name: Exercise Induced Collapse (EIC)
conditions.description: Can be inherited from the Labrador parent. DNA testing of the Labrador parent identifies carriers and affected animals.

lineage.name: Labradoodle
lineage.note: Coined in Australia in the 1980s, first bred as a low-shedding guide dog by crossing the Labrador with the Poodle.

children.name: Labrador
children.note: Brings the steady, friendly working temperament.

children.name: St John's Water Dog
children.note: The earlier dog it all grew from: the fishermen's water dog of Newfoundland, brought to Britain and bred up from there. Now extinct.

children.name: Fishermen's water dogs
children.note: The working water dogs the European fishing crews brought across the Atlantic.

children.name: Newfoundland landrace dogs
children.note: The local island dogs they crossed with once they landed. Now extinct.

children.name: British Pointers
children.note: Crossed in by British breeders for nose and a steady, focused drive in the field. Now in-decline.

children.name: British Setters
children.note: Added biddability and a love of working close with people on the shoot. Now in-decline.

children.name: Water spaniels
children.note: A touch of spaniel for a soft mouth and real keenness in water and cover. Now extinct.

children.name: Poodle
children.note: Brings the low-shedding coat that started the whole idea.

children.name: Old European water dogs
children.note: The rough water-retrieving dogs of Germany and France that fetched waterfowl for hunters. Now extinct.

children.name: Barbet and water spaniels
children.note: The curly-coated Barbet, the old water dog at the root of the whole poodle and bichon family. Now extinct.

name: Yorkshire Terrier
subtitle: Seven pounds of pure determination
fact: bred to catch mice and rats
character: Tenacious, feisty, brave, and sometimes bossy but eager to please. Can live into their late teens. Breed was gradually miniaturised

training.label: Bright but stubborn
traits: Intelligent terrier brain that picks things up quickly
Can be wilful and easily distracted by scent and movement
Responds best to short, positive, food-rewarded sessions
training.goodFor: Trick training, agility and obedience where their quick minds shine
training.watchOut: House training can be a challenge -- crate training from the start is strongly recommended

health.generalNote: Yorkshire Terriers are prone to several conditions typical of toy breeds including dental problems and luxating patella. Liver shunts are a serious concern in the breed.

conditions.name: Portosystemic shunt (liver shunt)
conditions.description: An abnormal blood vessel bypasses the liver, allowing toxins to accumulate in the bloodstream causing neurological signs, stunted growth and episodes of weakness. Surgical correction can be curative.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness and hopping gait. Mild cases are managed conservatively; severe cases require surgical correction.

conditions.name: Dental disease
conditions.description: Small jaws cause overcrowding of teeth leading to tartar build-up, gum disease and tooth loss from an early age. Daily tooth brushing and professional dental cleaning are essential.

conditions.name: Tracheal collapse
conditions.description: Weakening of the tracheal rings causes the airway to collapse during breathing, producing a characteristic honking cough. Harnesses instead of collars reduce pressure on the neck.

conditions.name: Hypoglycaemia
conditions.description: Very small Yorkshire Terrier puppies are vulnerable to dangerously low blood sugar, causing weakness, trembling and seizures. Frequent small meals are essential in young puppies.

famousDogs.name: Smoky
famousDogs.knownFor: Second World War therapy and war dog

lineage.name: Yorkshire Terrier
lineage.note: A working dog before a lap dog. Scottish weavers brought their terriers to the Yorkshire mills in the 1800s and crossed them with local ratters.

children.name: Paisley Terrier
children.note: A long-coated Scottish terrier that gave the silky, flowing coat.

children.name: Skye terrier stock
children.note: The long-coated Scottish terriers it was bred for beauty from. Now endangered.

children.name: Old Highland terriers
children.note: The working Scottish terriers behind the silky Clydeside strains. Now extinct.

children.name: Skye Terrier
children.note: Another Scottish terrier behind the long, fine hair.

children.name: Old Highland terriers
children.note: The old working-terrier stock of the Highlands that every Scottish terrier springs from. Now extinct.

children.name: Skye terrier stock
children.note: The long-coated Isle of Skye earth dogs at the root of the whole Highland terrier family. Now endangered.

children.name: Old English Black and Tan Terrier
children.note: Local ratting terriers that brought the size down and the colour in.

name: Poodle
subtitle: The most intelligent dog hiding in plain sight
fact: ranked second most intelligent of all dog
character: Three size varieties; Standard, Miniature, Toy. Springy & lively. Hunting dogs in elegant attire. Can be aggressive to people or other dogs.
coatColour: Silver, Brown, Black, Blue, Gray, White
coatLength: Short tight and curly / Dense and curly

training.label: Highly intelligent
traits: Learns rapidly and retains commands well
Adapts easily to different training styles
Excels at trick training and problem-solving tasks
training.goodFor: Agility, obedience, circus-style tricks, therapy work
training.watchOut: Can become bored with repetitive drills -- vary sessions to keep engagement high

health.generalNote: Poodles of all sizes are generally a healthy breed with good longevity but carry some significant inherited conditions including Addison's disease and bloat in Standards. Health testing is advisable.

conditions.name: Addison's disease
conditions.description: The adrenal glands fail to produce sufficient cortisol and aldosterone, causing episodes of weakness, vomiting and potentially fatal collapse. Manageable with lifelong hormone supplementation.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: Standard Poodles are among the at-risk breeds for GDV due to their deep chest. The stomach fills with gas and twists, requiring emergency surgery.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary retinal degeneration leading to blindness. DNA testing is available for several variants and should be carried out on all breeding animals.

conditions.name: Sebaceous Adenitis
conditions.description: An inflammatory skin disease destroying the sebaceous glands, causing scaling, hair loss and a musty odour. Requires long-term management with medicated shampoos and supplements.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis. Standard Poodles are most affected. Hip scoring of breeding animals is recommended.

lineage.name: Poodle
lineage.note: Now a clever companion and show dog, but built as a water-retrieving gundog. The name comes from the German 'Pudel', meaning to splash about.

children.name: Barbet and water spaniels

name: Dachshund
subtitle: Long on charm, short on compromise
fact: hunted badgers, the smaller dogs hunted ferrets
character: Two size varieties; Standard & Miniature. Independent & stubborn. Courageous & clever. A challenge to train. Prone to back problems.

training.label: Clever but stubborn
traits: Intelligent scent hound with a strong independent streak
House training can be particularly challenging and lengthy
Responds better to very high-value food rewards than praise alone
training.goodFor: Nose work and tracking activities that suit their hunting heritage
training.watchOut: House training requires exceptional patience and a strict routine from day one

health.generalNote: Dachshunds are highly susceptible to spinal disease due to their elongated body. Prevention through weight management and limiting jumping is as important as veterinary care.

conditions.name: Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
conditions.description: The most significant health risk for Dachshunds. Degenerated spinal discs rupture and press on the spinal cord, causing pain, paralysis or incontinence. Surgery can restore function if performed quickly.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary degeneration of the retina leading to blindness. DNA testing is available for some variants. Miniature long-haired Dachshunds are at highest risk.

conditions.name: Lafora Disease
conditions.description: A hereditary epilepsy affecting Miniature Wire-haired Dachshunds causing myoclonic jerks and seizures triggered by light, sound and movement. DNA testing identifies carriers.

conditions.name: Cushing's disease
conditions.description: Overproduction of cortisol causing increased thirst, pot belly, hair loss and muscle weakness. Manageable with medication but requires lifetime monitoring.

conditions.name: Obesity
conditions.description: Even modest excess weight dramatically increases stress on the spine and accelerates IVDD. Weight management is the single most important preventive measure for this breed.

lineage.name: Dachshund
lineage.note: The German badger dog, a scenthound bred low and long to follow its quarry straight down into the earth.

children.name: German bracke scenthounds

children.name: Old earth terriers
children.note: Terrier type earth dogs thought to add the grit for going to ground after badger and fox. Now extinct.

name: Springer Spaniel

Thrives with a job to do alongside family life

training.watchOut: Boundless energy means exercise before training is essential for focus

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)

conditions.name: Phosphofructokinase Deficiency (PFK)

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia

conditions.name: Ear infections
conditions.description: Long pendulous ears create the ideal environment for recurring infections. Regular cleaning and monitoring are essential for this breed throughout life.

conditions.name: Rage syndrome
conditions.description: Sudden, explosive and unpredictable aggression with no apparent trigger, followed by apparent confusion. More common in certain lines. A serious welfare and safety concern when present.

name: Border Collie
subtitle: The canine world's greatest overachiever
fact: Holder of many world records
character: Alert, agile and incredibly quick, mentally and physically. Highly intelligent, with an instinct to work. Thrive in active, outdoorsy households.

training.label: Exceptionally quick
traits: One of the most intelligent breeds -- learns new commands in very few repetitions
Needs mental stimulation as much as physical exercise
Can anticipate commands before you give them
training.goodFor: Agility, flyball, herding trials, advanced obedience
training.watchOut: Boredom leads to destructive behaviour -- training must be a daily commitment

health.generalNote: Border Collies are generally a healthy breed but carry several hereditary conditions including Collie Eye Anomaly and MDR1 drug sensitivity. DNA testing before breeding is essential.

conditions.name: Collie Eye Anomaly (CEA)
conditions.description: A hereditary eye condition present from birth affecting the development of the choroid and retina. Severity ranges from mild with no vision impact to severe detachment causing blindness. DNA testing is available.

conditions.name: MDR1 / ABCB1 drug sensitivity
conditions.description: A genetic mutation that allows certain medications to cross the blood-brain barrier, causing severe neurological toxicity. Affected dogs must not receive ivermectin, certain chemotherapy drugs and several other common medications.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis and mobility issues. Hip scoring is recommended for breeding animals.

conditions.name: Epilepsy
conditions.description: Idiopathic epilepsy has a hereditary component in the breed. Seizures usually begin in young adulthood and can often be managed with medication.

conditions.name: Trapped Neutrophil Syndrome (TNS)
conditions.description: A fatal immune deficiency where white blood cells cannot leave the bone marrow, leaving puppies vulnerable to infection. DNA testing identifies carriers and affected dogs.

lineage.note: Widely called the cleverest dog of all, bred purely for the work of gathering and moving sheep on the hills.

children.name: Cumberland sheepdogs

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia

conditions.name: Rage syndrome
conditions.description: Sudden unpredictable aggression with the dog appearing confused afterwards. Associated with certain lines. The exact cause remains debated.

name: Labrador
subtitle: Britain's best friend, officially
fact: can detect medical conditions in humans
character: Friendly, outgoing, and high-spirited. A keen love of water. Good tempered, very agile. Strongly built, broad in skull & chest.

training.label: Eager to please
traits: Picks up commands quickly, often in just a few repetitions
Highly food-motivated making reward-based training very effective
Rarely stubborn and responds well to consistent positive reinforcement
training.goodFor: Obedience, assistance work, agility and scent detection
training.watchOut: Enthusiasm can tip into pulling on the lead and jumping up -- start loose-lead manners early

health.generalNote: Labradors are generally a robust breed but their popularity means certain hereditary conditions are well-documented. Hip and elbow screening of both parents before purchase is strongly recommended.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal development of the hip joint leads to arthritis and mobility problems. Can range from mild stiffness to severe lameness requiring surgery. Both parents should be hip-scored before breeding.

conditions.name: Elbow dysplasia
conditions.description: A group of developmental conditions affecting the elbow joint, causing pain and lameness in one or both front legs. Surgical intervention is often required in moderate to severe cases.

conditions.name: Obesity
conditions.description: Labradors are famously food-motivated and prone to weight gain. Obesity significantly worsens joint conditions and reduces lifespan. Careful portion control and regular exercise are essential throughout life.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: A hereditary eye condition causing gradual deterioration of the retina, eventually leading to blindness. Dogs adapt well but it is not treatable. DNA testing can identify carriers before breeding.

conditions.name: Exercise Induced Collapse (EIC)
conditions.description: During intense exercise some Labs experience sudden muscle weakness and collapse. Episodes usually resolve within 30 minutes. DNA testing identifies affected and carrier dogs.

conditions.name: Centronuclear Myopathy (CNM)
conditions.description: A hereditary muscle disorder causing progressive weakness and muscle wasting. Symptoms appear in early puppyhood. DNA testing is available and responsible breeders screen for it.

conditions.name: Tricuspid Valve Dysplasia (TVD)
conditions.description: A congenital heart defect affecting the valve between the right atrium and ventricle. Severity varies widely -- some dogs are mildly affected while severe cases can cause heart failure.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus is a life-threatening emergency where the stomach fills with gas and twists. Requires immediate veterinary intervention. Large, deep-chested dogs are at higher risk.

name: Jack Russell Terrier
subtitle: Maximum mischief in minimum space
fact: Bred by the Reverend John Russell in the 1820s — a bold fox-bolting terrier that could keep up with hounds all day
character: Fearless, lively and incredibly smart — a big personality packed into a small, sturdy body
cons: Can be aggressive to other dogs
lookFor: Small, mostly white terrier with tan or black patches, a compact wiry body and an alert, intelligent expression
coatColour: White with tan, black or brown markings
coatLength: Short and smooth, or rough and broken

training.label: Feisty and wilful
traits: Extremely intelligent but strongly self-directed
Learns quickly -- both good and bad habits
Responds best to short, high-energy, reward-rich sessions
training.goodFor: Agility, earthdog sports and trick training where their energy works for you
training.watchOut: Off-lead recall is a long-term project -- never assume it is reliable near prey

health.generalNote: Jack Russell Terriers are a generally hardy breed with good longevity. Their main health concerns are lens luxation and Legg-Perthes disease, which should be screened in breeding animals.

conditions.name: Primary Lens Luxation (PLL)
conditions.description: The lens of the eye dislocates from its normal position, causing acute pain and glaucoma. Requires emergency treatment to save the eye. DNA testing is available and essential for breeding animals.

conditions.name: Legg-Calve-Perthes Disease
conditions.description: Degeneration of the femoral head causes hip pain and lameness in young dogs. Surgical removal of the femoral head provides excellent long-term results.

conditions.name: Luxating patella
conditions.description: The kneecap slips out of position causing intermittent lameness. Surgical correction is available for moderate to severe cases.

conditions.name: Deafness
conditions.description: Hereditary deafness occurs in predominantly white Jack Russells. BAER testing is recommended for all white or predominantly white puppies.

lineage.name: Jack Russell Terrier
lineage.note: Bred by a hunting parson, the Reverend John Russell, in the early 1800s for a game little terrier that could bolt a fox and keep up with the hounds.

children.name: Fox Terrier

children.name: English White Terrier

name: Boxer
subtitle: Playful, loyal, and forever young at heart
fact: The Boxer is named for its habit of standing on its hind legs and sparring with its front paws — a playful fighter from the start
character: Loyal, boisterous and devoted — brilliant with children and one of the most affectionate of the large breeds
lookFor: A medium-large dog with a broad, square head, short pushed-in muzzle, fawn or brindle coat and a white chest flash
coatColour: Fawn or brindle, typically with white markings on the face, chest and paws

training.label: Enthusiastic learner
traits: Playful, energetic and genuinely keen to work with their owner
Responds very well to positive reward-based training
Their boisterous nature can make focus a challenge early on
training.goodFor: Obedience, agility and protection sport
training.watchOut: Their puppy energy lasts for years -- patience and consistency is essential throughout

health.generalNote: Boxers have significant health concerns including cancer and heart disease. They are also brachycephalic, which creates breathing restrictions. Thorough screening is essential before breeding.

conditions.name: Cancer
conditions.description: Boxers have one of the highest cancer rates of any breed, with mast cell tumours, lymphoma and brain tumours among the most common. Regular health checks from middle age are important.

conditions.name: Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC)
conditions.description: A potentially fatal heart arrhythmia unique to the breed causing sudden death or progressive heart failure. Annual cardiac screening and DNA testing are essential for breeding animals.

conditions.name: Aortic stenosis
conditions.description: A narrowing of the aortic valve causing reduced blood flow from the heart. Can range from mild to life-limiting. Cardiac examination of puppies and breeding animals is recommended.

conditions.name: Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS)
conditions.description: Restricted airways due to the Boxer's flat face limit exercise tolerance and increase heat sensitivity. Corrective surgery can improve airflow in severely affected dogs.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis and pain. Screening of breeding stock is recommended.

lineage.name: Boxer
lineage.note: Developed in Munich in the 1890s. The FCI names the small Brabant Bullenbeisser as the Boxer's immediate ancestor, with later influence from the English Bulldog to further shorten the muzzle. The result: square-headed, athletic and devoted.

children.name: Brabant Bullenbeisser
children.note: The Brabant Bullenbeisser was the smaller, athletic German catch dog whose broad bite, square build and slightly upturned muzzle formed the immediate foundation of the modern Boxer. The FCI explicitly identifies it as the Boxer's direct ancestor. Now extinct.

children.name: Great Bullenbeisser (Danziger Bullenbeisser)
children.note: The larger, heavier northern German bull-baiting dog from which the smaller Brabant variant descended. Standing around 60-65cm and 40-50kg, it was used to seize and hold boar, bear and bull by the muzzle. The Brabant line emerged as a smaller, more agile regional type. Now extinct.

children.name: Medieval Alaunts and catch dogs
children.note: European catch dogs descended from dogs brought west by the Alans and other steppe peoples in the early medieval period. Bred across the continent for size, grip and courage in the hunt and in war. Now extinct.

children.name: Early Germanic boar hunting dogs
children.note: Large, rough hunting dogs native to northern Europe, documented in Roman accounts of Germanic tribes. Bred to seize and hold boar and bear rather than chase -- the instinct that defined the Bullenbeisser type. When crossed with the incoming Alaunt lines they produced the distinctly German bull-baiting dog. Now extinct.

children.name: Old English Bulldog
children.note: The stocky bull-baiting dog of England, crossed into the Brabant line via documented cross-channel exchange of bull-baiting dogs in the 16th and 17th centuries. Contributed the shortened muzzle and broader head. Now extinct.

children.name: Mastiff and Alaunt war dogs
children.note: The big Molossers and the extinct Alaunt that gave it bulk and a broad jaw.

children.name: Old British bandogs
children.note: The heavy butcher's and baiting dogs of old England. Now extinct.

children.name: German Bullenbeisser types
children.note: A group of regional German catch and hunting dogs bred to seize and hold large prey — boar, bear, bull — by the muzzle. The larger types worked as boarhounds; the smaller Brabant line became the Boxer. Distinct from the taller, rangier German boarhound. Now extinct.

children.name: Medieval Alaunts and catch dogs
children.note: European catch dogs descended from dogs brought west by the Alans and other steppe peoples in the early medieval period. Bred across the continent for size, grip and courage in the hunt and in war. Now extinct.

children.name: Old German farm guards
children.note: Heavy-boned German estate and farm dogs that contributed size and territorial drive to the Bullenbeisser lines. Now extinct.

children.name: Old English Bulldog
children.note: The athletic bull-baiting Bulldog of the early 19th century — leaner and more active than today's breed. Crossed into the early Boxer to further shorten the muzzle and soften temperament. Now extinct.

children.name: English Mastiff
children.note: The ancient Molosser line of Britain. Gave the Bulldog its bone, bulk and broad head.

children.name: Ancient Molossers
children.note: The huge eastern war and guard dogs at the root of every mastiff. Now extinct.

children.name: Old British bandogs
children.note: The heavy chained guard dogs of early Britain. Now extinct.

children.name: Ancient Molossers
children.note: The common deep ancestor of all Mastiff-type dogs across Europe. Now extinct.

name: Irish Setter
subtitle: Red-coated wanderer with a joyful soul
fact: originally bred as a gundog to point and retrieve game
character: Outgoing, energetic and affectionate. Playful and good-natured well into adulthood. Needs plenty of exercise. Friendly with people and other dogs.

training.label: Enthusiastic but easily distracted
traits: Willing and loving but their energy makes focus difficult
Needs a lot of exercise before any training session
Responds well to upbeat, varied positive training
training.goodFor: Field work, agility and activities that burn their considerable energy
training.watchOut: Their enthusiasm means they can learn bad habits as fast as good ones -- be consistent

health.generalNote: Irish Setters are generally healthy but carry progressive retinal atrophy and have a predisposition to bloat. DNA testing for PRA has greatly reduced the prevalence of the condition in the breed.

conditions.name: Progressive Retinal Atrophy (PRA)
conditions.description: Hereditary retinal degeneration leading to night blindness and eventual total blindness. DNA testing is available and has significantly reduced prevalence in well-bred lines.

conditions.name: Bloat (GDV)
conditions.description: The deep chest of the Irish Setter creates elevated GDV risk. Feeding smaller meals and avoiding exercise for two hours after eating significantly reduces the risk.

conditions.name: Hip dysplasia
conditions.description: Abnormal hip development causing arthritis. Hip scoring of breeding animals is recommended as part of a responsible health programme.

conditions.name: Hypothyroidism
conditions.description: Underactive thyroid causing weight gain, lethargy and coat changes. Manageable with daily thyroid hormone supplementation.

conditions.name: Canine Leucocyte Adhesion Deficiency (CLAD)
conditions.description: A severe immune deficiency causing recurrent life-threatening infections. DNA testing eliminates this condition from breeding lines. Effectively eradicated in health-tested populations.

lineage.name: Irish Setter
lineage.note: The flashy red gundog of Ireland, bred to range wide and find game birds across open country.
